name: OC Purple Accountability
tagline: Every vote. Every meeting. Every margin matters.
mission:
Orange County is one of America's few genuinely purple counties. Voter registration: 36.4% D, 33.9% R, 23.3% NPP. The City of Orange is split 36.6% D to 34.8% R. The 2024 CD-45 race was decided by 581 votes. In this environment, information is power.

OC Purple Accountability tracks voting records and policy positions across Orange County's key governing bodies — the Board of Supervisors, City of Orange council, City of Irvine council, and state legislative delegation. We publish scorecards. We distribute candidate questionnaires. We attend meetings and track who votes how.

We exist because most OC residents have no idea what their elected officials actually do. They might know who they voted for, but they don't know how that person voted on the housing element, the county budget, or the homelessness plan. This information asymmetry benefits organized special interests and hurts everyone else.

The 2026 elections are unusually high-stakes: the Board of Supervisors majority (currently 3-2 D) hangs on Districts 4 and 5. Multiple open state legislative seats (SD-34, AD-68) are in play. City councils in both Orange and Irvine have consequential races. New congressional maps from Prop 50 reshape federal representation. And the Governor's race (Newsom termed out) determines who enforces California's housing mandates.

In close elections, informed voters are the most powerful political force. We make voters informed.
theory_of_change: In close elections, information asymmetry benefits organized special interests. Scorecards and questionnaires level the playing field by making voting records and policy positions accessible to every voter, not just insiders. When voters know what their representatives actually do — not just what they say — accountability follows. In a county where margins are razor-thin, even small improvements in voter information shift outcomes. A candidate who knows their votes are being tracked and published votes differently. A voter who sees a scorecard votes more deliberately. We create the feedback loop between government action and public knowledge that democracy requires but rarely has.

political_positioning.description: Centered and deliberately nonpartisan in framing. The accountability mission appeals across the political spectrum — conservatives want government transparency, progressives want officials held to their promises, and moderates want data-driven evaluation. Our scorecards measure actions against stated positions, not against an ideological standard. A Republican who votes consistently with their platform gets a high consistency score, same as a Democrat who does. The compass cloud is intentionally wide because our coalition spans the spectrum. We are not a partisan organization — we are a transparency organization that operates in a purple county.

key_policy_positions: Publish regular scorecards for all OC Board of Supervisors members tracking votes on key issues
Develop and distribute candidate questionnaires for all 2026 races — county, city, state, and federal
Track meeting attendance and voting records for City of Orange and City of Irvine councils
Make all scorecards and tracking data publicly available in accessible, shareable formats
Maintain nonpartisan framing — evaluate officials against their own stated positions and constituent interests
Use public records requests to obtain voting records, campaign finance data, and policy positions
Partner with Voice of OC and other media to amplify accountability findings
Host candidate forums in partnership with League of Women Voters Orange Coast
Track campaign finance and major donor influence across OC races
Publish election guides summarizing candidate positions for every race on OC ballots

active_campaigns.title: Board of Supervisors Majority Defense
active_campaigns.summary:
⚡ UPDATE (May 19, 2026): BALLOTS IN VOTERS' HANDS — 1.9M mailed starting May 2. Mail-in voting open. ⚡ VOTER REGISTRATION DEADLINE PASSED (May 18). 13 DAYS until June 2 primary.

⚡ NEW POLL DATA: Governor's race is a three-way tie — Becerra 19%, Hilton 17%, Steyer 17%. Democratic fragmentation risk remains acute.

⚡ CNN GOVERNOR'S DEBATE RECAP (May 5): The most combative debate yet. Becerra took the most hits after his polling surge — attacked on single-payer healthcare flip and McCluskie corruption scandal. Bianco dominated conservative messaging on immigration. No clear winner. Five-way Democratic fragmentation persists. Risk of two Republicans making top-two remains real.

⚡ UPDATED CAMPAIGN FINANCE (Form 460 filings, as of April 18):

District 4 (open seat — Chaffee termed out, 341,494 voters):
• Fred Jung: $347K cash on hand, spent $128K, raised $58K since Jan 1. Lincoln Club endorsed.
• Connor Traut: $117K cash on hand, spent $136K (HIGHEST SPENDING RATE), raised $29K. OC Dem Party endorsed.
• Rose Espinoza: $150K self-loan + $44K nonmonetary contributions via Rosie's Garage.
• Tim Shaw: Only $14K spent, $16K raised — BUT National Association of Realtors spending $125K+ in ads supporting him.

District 5 (436,228 voters: 38.1% R, 32.8% D):
• Katrina Foley (D): Spent $222K, cash on hand $225K+. Association of OC Deputy Sheriffs spending $213K in union advertising supporting her.
• Diane Dixon (R): Spent $117K, cash on hand $232K+. Lincoln Club spending $36K+ supporting Dixon, $18K supporting Vellema, $11.6K opposing Foley — $66K total Lincoln Club spend.

⚡ KEY INSIGHT: District 5 is attracting the most outside money in OC. Voice of OC reports 'most of that money is headed toward South Orange County.' The financial picture is competitive — Dixon slightly edges Foley in cash on hand ($232K vs $225K) but Foley has massive union support.

The board currently has a 3-2 Democratic majority. If Democrats lose EITHER seat, the board flips — affecting the $10.8B annual budget, homelessness policy, transit funding, and housing policy countywide.

**May 18 update:** Housing has become the dominant issue in the California governor's race, with YIMBY Action and Abundant Housing LA endorsing Tom Steyer. Democratic field fragmentation remains a risk. The next governor controls RHNA enforcement and the future of California's YIMBY legislative agenda. For OC Board of Supervisors races, candidates' alignment with state housing mandates is increasingly a litmus test. All ballots are now in voters' hands for the current election cycle.

**May 21 update:** 12 DAYS to June 2 primary. ⚡ NEW EVITARUS POLL (May 14-16): Hilton 22%, Becerra 21%, Steyer 15% — Hilton retakes the lead within margin of error. Becerra's former strategist Dana Williamson pleaded guilty May 14 to campaign theft — opponents weaponized it at that day's debate. Voter commitment: Hilton 73% locked in vs. Becerra 52%, Steyer 48%. ⚡ D4: OCEA endorsed Traut. ⚡ D5: Dixon endorsed by 3 sitting supervisors (Chaffee, Wagner, Nguyen). ⚡ PIT COUNT (May 19): OC homelessness down 13.7% — Foley can cite this against Dixon's crime/homelessness attacks. ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH fined $160K, escalating to $50K/month in June — enforcement stakes are high.

**May 23 update:** 10 DAYS to June 2 primary. FY2026-27 Recommended Budget released May 20 for BOS review. LAist and Daily Titan published comprehensive voter guides covering all BOS races. ⚡ EVITARUS POLL (May 14-16): Governor's race — Hilton 22%, Becerra 21%, Steyer 15%. Democratic fragmentation persists. Voter commitment: Hilton 73% locked in vs. Becerra 52%. ⚡ D4: Financial picture stable — Jung $347K, Traut $117K (but broadest institutional support with OCEA + OC Dem Party + Chaffee), Shaw with $125K+ Realtor backing. ⚡ D5: Dixon endorsed by 3 sitting supervisors. Financial edge nearly even (Dixon $232K vs Foley $225K cash), but Foley has $213K in deputy sheriff union advertising support. ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH compliance deadline May 28 (5 DAYS) — fines escalate to $50K/month.

**May 24 update:** 9 DAYS to June 2 primary. Ballots in voters' hands. D4: Jung $347K vs. Traut's broadest institutional support vs. Shaw's $125K Realtor backing. D5: Foley ($225K + $213K union support) vs. Dixon ($232K + $66K Lincoln Club). Governor's race dead heat continues. Huntington Beach compliance deadline May 28 (4 DAYS) — fines escalate to $50K/month.

**May 25 update:** 8 DAYS to June 2 primary. Memorial Day weekend — campaign intensity may dip briefly but mail ballots continue arriving. ⚡ D4: Saddleback Meadows approval (May 6, unanimous BOS vote to deny appeal against 181-home development) provides a concrete example of how the current board handles development conflicts — the next D4 supervisor inherits this precedent. ⚡ D5: LAist published a comprehensive voter guide identifying this as one of OC's most competitive races — D5 is the most heavily Republican supervisor district (38.1% R). ⚡ Huntington Beach compliance deadline May 28 (3 DAYS) — court-ordered fines escalate to $50K/month in June. The housing enforcement environment is a live campaign issue in both races. Governor's race three-way dead heat continues (Hilton 22%, Becerra 21%, Steyer 15%).

**May 26 update:** 7 DAYS to June 2 primary. Memorial Day — campaign intensity dips briefly but mail ballots continue arriving. ⚡ D4: Financial picture stable — Jung $347K, Traut broadest institutional support (OCEA + OC Dem Party + Chaffee), Shaw $125K+ Realtor backing. ⚡ D5: Foley ($225K + $213K union support) vs. Dixon ($232K + $66K Lincoln Club). ⚡ Huntington Beach compliance deadline May 28 (2 DAYS) — fines escalate to $50K/month. Governor's race dead heat continues. Final week of campaigning begins tomorrow.

**May 27 update:** 6 DAYS to June 2 primary. Final week of campaigning underway. OC is solidifying its 'purple county' status — every race is competitive. ⚡ D4: Heavy outside spending intensifies — NAR $125K for Shaw, Lincoln Club for Jung, OC Dem Party $76K for Traut. ⚡ D5: Union spending totals $565K for Foley; Lincoln Club $163K against Foley. This is the most expensive supervisor race in OC history. ⚡ OC PIT COUNT: Homelessness down 13.7% to 6,321 — first time more sheltered than unsheltered. Both D4/D5 winners inherit this progress and must sustain it. ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH: Compliance deadline TOMORROW (May 28) — $50K/month fines start June. $160K in total penalties ordered. ⚡ AD-68: Penaloza ($1M+ from tech) vs Lopez ($118K SEIU + $400K negative ads). ⚡ SD-34: Valencia vs Shader — straight D-vs-R. County budget pressures from Airport Fire payouts, sexual harassment settlements, and federal funding cuts make fiscal transparency a central issue across all races. ⚡ Supervisor Foley's herbicide use pause remains a local controversy in D5.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: 5 DAYS to June 2 primary. ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH COMPLIANCE DEADLINE IS TODAY — $50K/month fines start June. Housing enforcement is a live final-week campaign issue. NEW PPIC POLL: Becerra 23%, Hilton 20%, Steyer 15%, Bianco 13%, Porter 12% — Becerra leads for first time in most credible recent poll but still within margin of error. Five-way D fragmentation still risks two Republicans making top-two. D4: Jung ($347K) vs. Traut (OCEA + Dem Party) vs. Shaw ($125K NAR). D5: Foley ($225K + $565K union) vs. Dixon ($232K + $163K Lincoln Club) — most expensive supervisor race in OC history. Both races determine whether the 3-2 Democratic board majority holds.

**May 29 update:** ⚡ 4 DAYS to June 2 primary. ⚡ GARDEN GROVE CHEMICAL CRISIS DOMINATES FINAL WEEK: GKN Aerospace's methyl methacrylate tank failure forced evacuation of 50,000 residents in Garden Grove and Stanton (May 21-26). Governor Newsom declared state of emergency. BOS ratified federal emergency declaration. Class-action lawsuit filed. DA Spitzer launched criminal probe. Garden Grove/Stanton are IN DISTRICT 4 — this crisis is a direct test of emergency management capacity and shapes the D4 race in the final days. Candidates must articulate emergency preparedness and industrial accountability positions. ⚡ D4: Garden Grove crisis gives Traut (Buena Park Mayor, OC Dem Party + OCEA endorsed) and Jung (Fullerton Mayor, Lincoln Club, $347K war chest) a chance to demonstrate crisis leadership. Shaw's $125K NAR backing may not resonate against an industrial safety emergency. ⚡ D5: Foley ($225K + $565K union) vs. Dixon ($232K + $163K Lincoln Club) — most expensive supervisor race in OC history. ⚡ PPIC POLL (May 14-18): Becerra 23%, Hilton 20%, Steyer 15% — Becerra leads for first time in most credible poll. A Becerra-Hilton top-two is most likely. Five-way Dem fragmentation still risks two Republicans making top-two but appears less likely. ⚡ Huntington Beach compliance deadline passed yesterday (May 28) — $50K/month fines start June if noncompliant. This enforcement precedent remains a campaign issue.

**May 30 update:** ⚡ 3 DAYS to June 2 primary. Final weekend — in-person early voting opens at some locations. NEW UC BERKELEY IGS POLL (May 19-24): Becerra 25%, Hilton 21%, Steyer 19%. The race for second is TIGHTENING — Steyer within 2 points of Hilton, making a two-Democrat top-two plausible for the first time. ⚡ D4: Garden Grove crisis aftermath continues — Newsom initiated safety record review of GKN and chemical plants. Emergency management permanently embedded as D4 campaign issue. The three-way Democratic split (Jung/Traut/Espinoza) vs. lone Republican Shaw remains the decisive dynamic. ⚡ D5: Foley ($225K + $565K union) vs. Dixon ($232K + $163K Lincoln Club) — both advance to November in a two-person race, but the margin establishes momentum. Huntington Beach's May 28 compliance deadline passed — $50K/month fines start June. Both D4/D5 results determine the 3-2 Democratic board majority and the $10.8B budget.

⚡ MAY 31 UPDATE: 2 DAYS to June 2 primary. ALL VOTE CENTERS OPEN TODAY (10am-6pm) and tomorrow. Election Day June 2 (7am-8pm). ⚡ FINAL EMERSON POLL (May 27-28, n=1,000): Becerra 28%, Steyer 22%, Hilton 21%. Becerra extends lead; Steyer PASSES Hilton for the first time — a two-Democrat top-two (Becerra + Steyer) is now the most likely outcome. This would be favorable for housing enforcement and the progressive agenda. ⚡ HUNTINGTON BEACH COMPLIANCE CONFIRMED: California HCD indicates HB received state approval for its housing plan, ending the 4.5+ year noncompliance saga ($160K+ in penalties, $50K/month fines averted). The enforcement model works — even the most resistant jurisdictions comply under sustained pressure. ⚡ D4: Final weekend dynamic — three-way D/NPP split (Jung $347K/Traut broadest institutional support/Espinoza self-funded) vs. lone Republican Shaw ($125K NAR). Garden Grove crisis aftermath embedded. ⚡ D5: Foley ($225K + $565K union) vs. Dixon ($232K + $163K Lincoln Club) — both advance to November in two-person race, margin establishes momentum. ⚡ Santa Ana ICE moonlighting ordinance advancing — local governance issue across OC.

**June 1 update:** ⚡ PRIMARY IS TOMORROW (June 2). D4: Jung leads cash ($347K), Traut has Dem Party ($76K). D5: Foley vs Dixon — most expensive supervisor race in OC history. Foley has $565K+ union support. IF DIXON WINS NOVEMBER: board flips 3-2 Republican. D4/D5 outcomes set the battleground.

**June 3 update:** ⚡ JUNE 2 PRIMARY RESULTS — KEY OUTCOMES: D4: Shaw (R, 17,895) vs. Traut (D, 17,827) advance — 68-vote margin, ultra-tight. D5: Foley (D) leads Dixon (R). Governor: Hilton (R) 27% / Becerra (D) 26% — both likely advance. 21.7% turnout countywide. The D4 result sets up a direct partisan contest for November — if Shaw wins, the 3-2 Democratic BOS majority flips to Republican, affecting the $10.5B budget, housing enforcement, and the Garden Grove crisis response. The Andrew Do corruption scandal backdrop (former D1 supervisor) emphasizes accountability for all candidates. DA Spitzer’s criminal investigation into GKN Aerospace expanding.

**June 4 update:** UPDATED VOTE COUNTS: D4 — Shaw (R) 18,794 vs Traut (D) 18,304 (490-vote margin, narrower than election night). D5 — Foley (D) 48,745 vs Dixon (R) 46,256. D2 — Sarmiento dominates 28,630 vs Davis 6,738. Governor — Hilton vs Becerra too close to call. 21.7% turnout. Certification deadline July 10. Mail-in ballots postmarked by June 2 accepted through June 9.

**June 5 update:** ⚡ UPDATED COUNTS (canvass ongoing, 57% of expected vote counted statewide): D4 — Shaw (R) 18,794 vs Traut (D) 18,304 (490-vote margin). D5 — Foley (D) 48,745 vs Dixon (R) 46,256. D2 — Sarmiento dominates 28,630 vs Davis 6,738. ⚡ GOVERNOR: Hilton (R) 27%, Becerra (D) 26%. Katie Porter conceded. Matt Mahan and Villaraigosa dropped out. Top two advance to November — certification by July 3. ⚡ The November general election matchups are now set: D4 Shaw (R) vs Traut (D) is a direct partisan contest for the 3-2 Democratic BOS majority. D5 Foley (D) vs Dixon (R) determines whether the majority holds. If Democrats lose EITHER seat, the board flips. Canvass period through July 10. Post-primary analysis and November strategy development should begin immediately.

**June 7 update:** ⚡ DOUBLE JEOPARDY: Dixon (R) has TAKEN THE LEAD over Foley (D) in D5 — latest count: Dixon 62,640 (48.5%) vs Foley 58,654 (45.4%). Combined with Shaw (R) leading Traut (D) by ~1,600 votes in D4 (26,264 to 24,643), the 3-2 Democratic BOS majority now faces potential defeat in BOTH swing seats. If both Republicans win in November, the board flips 4-1 Republican. Governor race: Becerra (D) takes slight lead over Hilton (R) — 26.7% vs 26.4%. SD-34: Valencia (D) dominant at 63% vs Shader (R) 37%. Canvass continues through July 10. The scorecard and voter accountability work is now mission-critical for November.

**June 8 update:** June 8 is a scheduled canvass vote count update day. As of latest counts: D4 — Shaw (R) 26,264 vs Traut (D) 24,643 (~1,621-vote gap). D5 — Dixon (R) 62,640 vs Foley (D) 58,654 (~4,000-vote gap). TOMORROW (June 9) is the last day for mail ballot receipt (postmarked by June 2). Certification deadline July 10. Both seats trending Republican heading into November. Governor: Becerra (D) 26.7% vs Hilton (R) 26.4% — Katie Porter conceded. Developing the November general election strategy for BOTH D4 and D5 is now the top priority.

**June 9 update:** ⚡ TODAY is the LAST DAY for mail ballot receipt (postmarked by June 2). After today, no new ballots accepted — remaining count is from already-received ballots. D4: Shaw (R) leads Traut (D) by ~1,600+ votes. D5: Dixon (R) leads Foley (D) ~48.5% to 45.4%. Both seats trending Republican heading into November. Certification deadline July 10. The window for reversing these trends is the November general election campaign — developing strategy for BOTH D4 and D5 is now the urgent priority. Governor: Becerra (D) has overtaken Hilton (R) 26.7% to 26.4%.

**June 10 update:** Mail ballot deadline PASSED yesterday — remaining count is from finite pool of already-received ballots. D4: Shaw (R) leads 26,264 (33.27%) vs Traut (D) 24,643 (31.22%) — margin ~1,600, November runoff certain. D5: Dixon (R) leads 62,640 (48.5%) vs Foley (D) 58,654 (45.4%) — gap widened to ~4,000 votes. Voice of OC: 'Is an Incumbent Going to Lose Reelection?' Both seats trending Republican heading into November. OC Registrar posting daily 5 PM updates through June 26. Certification July 10. Developing November strategy for BOTH D4 and D5 is now the urgent priority. Governor: Becerra (D) 26.7% vs Hilton (R) 26.4%, both advance.

**June 11 update:** OC Registrar posting updated vote counts today at 5 PM. D4: Shaw (R) leads 26,264 (33.27%) vs Traut (D) 24,643 (31.22%) — margin ~1,600. D5: Dixon (R) leads 62,640 (48.5%) vs Foley (D) 58,654 (45.4%) — gap ~4,000 votes. Mail ballot receipt deadline passed — all remaining counting from already-received ballots. ⚡ FBI/EPA served federal search warrant at GKN Aerospace yesterday (June 10) — the federal criminal probe adds a new dimension to D4 accountability issues. Next count updates: June 12, 16, 18, 24, 26. Certification July 10. November strategy for BOTH D4 and D5 is now the urgent priority. HB housing vote June 16 (5 DAYS).

**June 12 update:** ⚡ MAJOR REVERSAL: Democrats have pulled ahead in BOTH supervisor races. D4: Traut (D) now leads Shaw (R) with 33%+ vs 31%+ — flipped from election night. D5: Foley (D) leads Dixon (R) 46.98% to 46.81% — razor-thin margin after Dixon led since election night. Voice of OC headline: 'Democrats Pull Ahead in Orange County Supervisor Races.' Late mail ballots skewing Democratic, consistent with California trends. Only ~10,000 ballots remaining countywide. OC Registrar posting update today at 5 PM. Certification July 10. If these leads hold, Democrats maintain the 3-2 majority going into November with momentum. HB housing vote June 16 (4 DAYS). FBI raid fallout at GKN continues — GKN committed $5M total in community support.

**June 13 update:** ⚡ D5 race continues to SEESAW — Dixon (R) appears to have retaken the lead from Foley (D) at ~48% vs ~46% in the latest counts, reversing the brief Democratic lead reported June 10. The lead has changed hands multiple times since election night. Only ~10K ballots remaining countywide. D4: Traut (D) maintains lead at 33%+ vs Shaw (R) 31%+. Certification July 10. If Dixon holds through November, Republicans retake the 3-2 majority. OC Registrar updates at 5 PM weekdays; next scheduled updates June 16, 18, 24, 26. GKN SVP attended June 9 council meeting — dodged reimbursement questions; FBI served search warrant. HB housing vote June 16 (3 DAYS).

**June 14 update:** D5: Dixon (R) has CONSOLIDATED lead at ~48.5% vs Foley (D) ~45.4% — only ~9,600 ballots remaining countywide. After Foley briefly led around June 10, the remaining ballots proved more evenly split. D4: Shaw (R) leads Traut (D) ~33% vs ~31%. OC Registrar next update Monday June 16 at 5 PM. Certification July 10. If Dixon holds through November, Republicans retake the 3-2 majority. SBA Business Recovery Center for GKN crisis opening June 16 at 12966 Euclid St, Garden Grove. HB housing vote June 16 (2 DAYS).

**June 15 update:** D5: Dixon (R) lead steady at ~48.5% vs Foley (D) ~45.4% — ~9,600 ballots remaining. OC Registrar next update TOMORROW (June 16) at 5 PM — first count since June 12. D4: Shaw (R) at ~33.3% vs Traut (D) ~31.2%. Certification July 10. HB housing vote TOMORROW (June 16). SBA Business Recovery Center opens TOMORROW at 12966 Euclid St, Garden Grove.

**June 16 update:** OC Registrar posting updated ballot count TODAY at 5 PM — first count since June 12. D5: Dixon (R) at ~48.5% vs Foley (D) ~45.4%. D4: Shaw (R) at ~33.3% vs Traut (D) ~31.2%. Only ~9,600 ballots remaining countywide. Certification July 10. HB housing element vote TODAY. SBA Business Recovery Center OPENS TODAY at 12966 Euclid St, Garden Grove.

**June 17 update:** ⚡ D5: Foley (D) reclaims narrow lead — 47.0% (85,104) vs Dixon (R) 46.8% (84,777). Margin: 327 votes with ~9,600 ballots remaining. D4: Shaw (R) ~33% vs Traut (D) ~31% — both advance. Next OC Registrar count June 18. Certification July 10. First general election poll: Becerra (D) 52% vs Hilton (R) 31% — Democratic strength could boost Foley in November. Garden Grove council meeting June 23.

**June 18 update:** D5: Foley (D) 47.0% vs Dixon (R) 46.8% — margin 327 votes. OC Registrar count TODAY ~5 PM; ~9,500 ballots remaining. Both advance to November. D4: Shaw (R) ~33% vs Traut (D) ~31% — both advance. ⚡ Huntington Beach housing element APPROVED 6-1 on June 16 — city finally zones for 13,000+ residences after years of defiance. Certification deadline July 10. Garden Grove council meeting June 23.

**June 19 update:** ⚡ ALERT: LEAD CHANGE IN D5 — Dixon (R) has pulled ahead to ~48.96% vs Foley (D) at ~45.08% in latest OC Registrar count. This reverses Foley's earlier slim lead and threatens the 3-2 Democratic majority. Remaining ballots are cure-period and signature-verification returns — pool nearly exhausted. Both advance to November regardless. Additional counts June 24, 26. Certification July 10. D4: Shaw (R) vs Traut (D) — both advancing. Becerra (D) +21 over Hilton (R) in first general poll could boost Democratic turnout in November, but primary margin sets the narrative.

**June 20 update:** No OC Registrar update today — next count June 24. D5: Dixon (R) leads Foley (D) ~48.96% to ~45.08%. D4: Shaw (R) vs Traut (D) — both advancing. Remaining ballots nearly exhausted. Certification July 10. Grand Jury homelessness response deadline June 30 (10 DAYS). Becerra (D) +21 over Hilton (R) in first general poll could boost November Democratic turnout.

**June 21 update:** ⚡ D5 FLIPPED: Foley (D) now leads Dixon (R) ~47.0% to ~46.8% in latest count — dramatic reversal. ~10,000 ballots remaining. Both advance to November (no candidate above 50%). Next count June 24 (3 DAYS). Certification July 10. Grand Jury homelessness response deadline June 30 (9 DAYS). Becerra (D) +21 over Hilton (R) in first general governor poll — strong Democratic headwinds for November.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: Board of Supervisors races are low-information, low-turnout elections where organized advocacy has outsized impact. Most voters can't name their supervisor. A clear, nonpartisan voter guide that evaluates candidates on specific policy positions — housing, budget, homelessness, transit — helps voters make informed choices in a race they'd otherwise skip or vote randomly. When the margin of victory is measured in thousands of votes (or less), even modest improvements in informed voting shift outcomes.
next_actions: Profile all four District 4 candidates (Jung, Traut, Espinoza, Shaw) — backgrounds, positions, endorsements, donors
Deep-dive District 5: compare Foley's record as supervisor vs Dixon's Assembly voting record on housing, transit, budget
Develop and distribute candidate questionnaire to all six candidates across both districts
Create voter guide comparing candidates on housing, budget, homelessness, transit, and transparency — publish by May 1
Track campaign finance filings — Jung's $197K war chest vs others creates an early narrative worth publicizing
Coordinate with Voice of OC to amplify candidate evaluation and campaign finance coverage
Organize candidate forum in partnership with League of Women Voters Orange Coast — target late April/early May
Publish final voter guide at least 4 weeks before June 2 primary
Develop D4 + D5 November general election voter accountability strategy — both seats now at risk
Track Dixon's housing and homelessness positions for the general election scorecard
Monitor canvass period through July 10 for final certified results

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of Board of Supervisors Majority Defense often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Define clear outcomes, timelines, and guardrails before scaling spending or mandates.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Campaigns with published milestones and public reporting earn broader support and allow mid-course corrections.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If Board of Supervisors Majority Defense is worth doing, it is worth measuring with transparent benchmarks so everyone can see what is working."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Implement change in ways that reduce disruption and share benefits across existing residents and newcomers.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Phased implementation and regular public feedback often lower backlash while improving policy durability.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We can move Board of Supervisors Majority Defense forward while still listening to neighborhood-level concerns and adjusting implementation details."

common_ground: Public policy should solve real problems, not just signal values.
Residents deserve clear communication about tradeoffs.
Programs should be adjusted when evidence shows gaps.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Assuming all critics are acting in bad faith.
Overpromising speed or certainty on complex implementation.
Using national partisan frames for local decisions.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: When baseline data is incomplete or lagged.
When legal or budget decisions are still pending.
When implementation depends on partner agencies outside our control.
honest_limits: This campaign cannot alone fix every upstream structural problem.
We cannot guarantee identical outcomes in every neighborhood.
We rely on sustained public participation and institutional follow-through.

active_campaigns.title: City of Orange Council Scorecard
active_campaigns.summary:
Track and publish the voting records of all City of Orange council members on key issues — housing (especially Housing Element compliance and Builder's Remedy projects), budget, development, and public safety. The scorecard will be published before the November 2026 elections when 3 council seats and the mayor are on the ballot. Orange's nonpartisan elections make information even more critical — without party labels, voters rely on name recognition or random choice. A scorecard breaks through that.

**June 1 update:** PRIMARY TOMORROW. SD-34 (Valencia vs Shader), AD-68 (Peñaloza $407K vs Lopez $224K), CA Governor (Becerra leads). Governor outcome affects housing enforcement. Huntington Beach achieved compliance — state enforcement works. Orange NOT on noncompliant list.

**June 4 update:** Primary complete. November races for 3 council seats + mayor set. Housing Element compliance maintained.

**June 5 update:** Primary complete. November races set: 3 council seats + mayor. Huntington Beach delayed housing element vote to June 16 — enforcement environment remains active. Scorecard research should accelerate during summer to publish before November filing.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: In nonpartisan city elections, voters have even less information than in partisan races. A well-researched scorecard that tracks actual votes (not campaign promises) gives voters concrete data to evaluate incumbents and compare them to challengers. Council members who know their votes are being tracked and published have an incentive to take positions publicly rather than hiding behind procedural votes.
next_actions: Obtain City of Orange council meeting minutes for the past 12 months
Identify key votes on housing, budget, and development issues
Design scorecard methodology — what votes to include, how to weight them
Research November 2026 council candidate field (3 seats + mayor)
Draft scorecard format for public distribution
Partner with Voice of OC for coverage of scorecard findings

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of City of Orange Council Scorecard often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Define clear outcomes, timelines, and guardrails before scaling spending or mandates.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Campaigns with published milestones and public reporting earn broader support and allow mid-course corrections.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If City of Orange Council Scorecard is worth doing, it is worth measuring with transparent benchmarks so everyone can see what is working."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Implement change in ways that reduce disruption and share benefits across existing residents and newcomers.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Phased implementation and regular public feedback often lower backlash while improving policy durability.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We can move City of Orange Council Scorecard forward while still listening to neighborhood-level concerns and adjusting implementation details."

common_ground: Public policy should solve real problems, not just signal values.
Residents deserve clear communication about tradeoffs.
Programs should be adjusted when evidence shows gaps.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Assuming all critics are acting in bad faith.
Overpromising speed or certainty on complex implementation.
Using national partisan frames for local decisions.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: When baseline data is incomplete or lagged.
When legal or budget decisions are still pending.
When implementation depends on partner agencies outside our control.
honest_limits: This campaign cannot alone fix every upstream structural problem.
We cannot guarantee identical outcomes in every neighborhood.
We rely on sustained public participation and institutional follow-through.

active_campaigns.title: Irvine Council Scorecard
active_campaigns.summary:
Track and publish voting records for Irvine City Council, especially on the General Plan update (57,656 units proposed), Irvine Company-related decisions, and Great Park development. The 5-2 Democratic majority is at risk in November 2026 (3 seats + mayor on ballot). Irvine's decisions on housing density affect the entire OC housing market. The Irvine Company's monopoly power makes transparency especially important — track council votes on matters where the Irvine Company has a financial interest.

**June 1 update:** Planning Commission voted 7-0 in March for Oak Creek zoning amendment. Irvine Company revised: 50+ acre park + 3,100 homes. Council to hear item. Possible ballot measure but not scheduled. November elections (3 seats + mayor) critical.

**June 4 update:** Oak Creek zoning amendment approved April 14 by council. 50+ acre park reduces units to under 3,000. Opposition gathering signatures for ballot initiative. November: 3 seats + mayor.

**June 5 update:** OC Streetcar delayed to March 2027. Oak Creek zoning amendment approved April 14. Begin compiling council voting records for scorecard — November elections (3 seats + mayor) approaching.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: The Irvine Company's near-monopoly over Irvine's apartment market creates unique transparency needs. When one entity controls ~75% of a city's apartment units, every council vote on development policy is a potential conflict of interest. A public scorecard tracking how council members vote on Irvine Company-related items exposes these dynamics. Voters can then evaluate whether their representatives serve residents or developers.
next_actions: Research Irvine council voting patterns on General Plan and Irvine Company matters
Identify Irvine Company-related agenda items over the past 12 months
Design scorecard methodology tracking housing, Irvine Company, and budget votes
Track November 2026 Irvine council candidates' positions and funding sources
Coordinate with People for Housing OC on data sharing

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Reasonable skeptics of Irvine Council Scorecard often worry about unintended costs, fairness across neighborhoods, and whether promised benefits will be measured transparently rather than assumed.

active_campaigns.title: State Legislative Open-Seat Tracking
active_campaigns.summary:
UPDATE (May 17, 2026): Candidate fields are set for all races. Ballots in voters' hands. 13 DAYS until June 2 primary. ⚡ VOTER REGISTRATION DEADLINE PASSED (May 18).

SD-34 (Umberg termed out): Straight D-vs-R contest: Avelino Valencia (D, current AD-68 Assemblymember) vs. Rhonda Shader (R, former Placentia mayor, 2022 runner-up). Both will advance from the June 2 top-two primary to the November general.

AD-68 (Valencia running for Senate): Jessie Lopez (D, Santa Ana councilor, endorsed by CA Legislative Women's Caucus, raised $73,737) vs. David Peñaloza (D, Santa Ana councilor, endorsed by Valencia, raised $142,897). All-Democratic field means the primary decides the seat.

⚡ GOVERNOR'S RACE — CNN DEBATE RECAP (May 5): Most combative yet. Becerra took heavy fire from all sides after polling surge. Bianco dominated conservative messaging. No clear winner — five-way D fragmentation persists. Ballots already being cast. Risk of two Republicans making top-two remains real.

Swalwell exited — name stays on June 2 ballot. Newsom not endorsing.

State legislators shape California's housing mandate framework (RHNA, Builder's Remedy, SB 9/10 enforcement) that drives the core YIMBY fight in OC.

**May 27 update:** 6 DAYS to June 2 primary. ⚡ AD-67: Pulido vs Briceno — millions in special interest spending. ⚡ AD-68: Outside money dominates — Penaloza has $1M+ from tech interests; Lopez has $118K from SEIU plus $400K in negative ads against Penaloza. ⚡ SD-34: Valencia (D) vs Shader (R) — both advance, but the primary sets the narrative for November. OC's 'purple county' shift makes every state legislative race consequential for the housing mandate framework.

⚡ MAY 28 UPDATE: 5 DAYS to June 2 primary. SD-34: Valencia (D) vs. Shader (R) advance to November. AD-68: All-Democratic primary — Peñaloza ($1M+ tech support, $142K raised) vs. Lopez ($118K + $400K negative ads). Governor's race: NEW PPIC POLL shows Becerra 23%, Hilton 20%, Steyer 15%. CalMatters published last-minute voter guide for undecided voters.

**May 29 update:** ⚡ 4 DAYS to June 2 primary. Final week of campaigning. PPIC poll (May 14-18): Becerra 23%, Hilton 20%, Steyer 15%, Bianco 13%, Porter 12%. Becerra's surge driven by consolidation of Democratic voters after Swalwell's exit — 39% of Dems now back Becerra. Hilton locked in at 73% voter commitment vs. Becerra 52%. The Garden Grove chemical crisis (50K evacuated, state of emergency) adds an emergency management dimension to the governor's race — Newsom declared the emergency, testing how candidates respond to industrial safety. AD-68 and SD-34 races proceed to their conclusions.

**June 1 update:** ⚡ PRIMARY TOMORROW. SD-34: Valencia vs Shader — both advance. AD-68: Peñaloza ($407K) vs Lopez ($224K), heavy outside spending. CD-40: DEAD HEAT — Calvert 24%, Kim 22%, Varet (D) 20%. Governor: Becerra 23-28%, Steyer vs Hilton dead heat.

**June 4 update:** SD-34 — Valencia (D) dominates 63% vs Shader (R) 37%. Both advance to November. AD-68 results pending (Peñaloza vs Lopez). Governor — Hilton (R) vs Becerra (D) too close to call. Certification by July 10.

**June 5 update:** ⚡ UPDATED PRIMARY RESULTS (canvass ongoing): SD-34 — Valencia (D) 63% vs Shader (R) 37%; both advance. AD-68 — Peñaloza vs Lopez final count pending. ⚡ GOVERNOR: Hilton (R) 27%, Becerra (D) 26% with 57% counted. Katie Porter conceded. Certification by July 3. A Hilton vs Becerra general election is the most likely outcome — the most consequential housing enforcement race in California in years. The next governor controls RHNA enforcement, Builder's Remedy, and AG referrals.

**June 7 update:** SD-34 results now clear: Valencia (D) 63% vs Shader (R) 37% — Valencia is the heavy favorite for the November general given the district's Democratic lean. Governor: Becerra (D) takes slight lead over Hilton (R) as ballots continue to be counted. Both advance to November. The governor's race determines the state housing enforcement environment — scorecard integration needed.

**June 8 update:** Governor race update: Katie Porter conceded. AP projected Becerra advances. Hilton vs Steyer race for second remains close but Hilton favored — Becerra 26.7%, Hilton 26.4%, Steyer ~21%. Certification deadline July 3. SD-34: Valencia (D) 63% vs Shader (R) 37% — Valencia is the heavy favorite for November.

**June 9 update:** Governor: Becerra (D) has overtaken Hilton (R) — 26.7% to 26.4%. Becerra rapidly closed the gap as mail-in ballots from Democratic counties arrived. TODAY is the last day for ballot receipt. Certification deadline July 3. Steyer at ~20%, has not conceded. SD-34: Valencia (D) 63% vs Shader (R) 37% — Valencia heavy favorite for November. The governor's race determines California's housing enforcement framework.

**June 10 update:** Governor: Becerra (D) 26.7% vs Hilton (R) 26.4% — ~65% of expected votes counted. AP, ABC7, NBC, CalMatters all project both advance to November. Mail ballot deadline passed yesterday. Steyer at ~21%. County certification deadline July 3. SD-34: Valencia (D) 63% vs Shader (R) 37% remains unchanged. First successful Builder's Remedy court case won in La Cañada Flintridge (80 units) — sets precedent for OC cities.

**June 11 update:** Governor: Becerra (D) 26.7% vs Hilton (R) 26.4% — AP, ABC7 confirm both advance to November. Counting continues; county certification deadline July 3. SD-34: Valencia (D) 63% vs Shader (R) 37% — Valencia heavy favorite in November. The governor determines California's housing enforcement posture for RHNA, Builder's Remedy, AG referrals.

**June 12 update:** Governor: Becerra (D) ~28% vs Hilton (R) ~25% at 88% of expected votes counted — gap WIDENING as late mail ballots are processed. Both confirmed advancing to November. County certification deadline July 3. ⚡ Democrats also pulling ahead in OC D4 and D5 — statewide Democratic momentum in late ballot counting. The governor determines California's housing enforcement posture for RHNA, Builder's Remedy, AG referrals.

**June 13 update:** Governor: Becerra (D) ~28% vs Hilton (R) ~25% at 88% counted — gap continues to widen. Hilton led on election night but was overtaken as mail ballots came in. County certification deadline July 3. D5 supervisor race seesawing — Dixon (R) appears to have retaken lead from Foley (D). HB housing vote June 16 (3 DAYS) — revised draft posted June 8; $50K/month fines accruing.

**June 14 update:** Governor: Becerra (D) extending lead — latest SoS data shows ~26.7% vs Hilton (R) ~26.4% at 65% counted; at 88% counted gap was ~28% vs ~25%. County certification deadline July 3. D5: Dixon (R) consolidated lead at ~48.5% vs Foley (D) ~45.4%. HB housing vote June 16 (2 DAYS) — $50K/month fines accruing; total owed now $260K+.

**June 15 update:** Governor: Becerra (D) at ~28% vs Hilton (R) ~25% with 88% counted. County certification deadline July 3. OC Registrar next update TOMORROW (June 16) at 5 PM. HB housing vote TOMORROW — $50K/month fines accruing; total owed now ~$310K+.

**June 16 update:** Governor: Becerra (D) ~26.7% vs Hilton (R) ~26.4% — extremely tight margin; AP confirmed both advancing to November on June 5. County certification deadline July 3. OC Registrar ballot update TODAY at 5 PM. HB housing element vote TODAY — $50K/month fines accruing; total owed now ~$335K+.

**June 17 update:** First general election poll: Becerra (D) 52% vs Hilton (R) 31% — commanding 21-point lead. Registered Democrats outnumber Republicans nearly 2-to-1 statewide. Final primary tallies (88% counted): Becerra ~28%, Hilton ~25%, Steyer ~23%. OC county certification deadline July 3. Next registrar count June 18. HB housing element: June 16 vote result not yet confirmed; fines ~$385K+ and climbing; could escalate to $100K/month in July.

**June 18 update:** CA Governor primary confirmed: Becerra (D) 28% vs Hilton (R) 25% advance to November. First general poll: Becerra +21 points. ⚡ Huntington Beach housing element APPROVED 6-1 on June 16 — Councilmember Chad Williams sole dissent. City zones for 13,000+ residences. Judge to rule next month on increasing fines from $50K to $150K/month; adoption may reduce future penalties. HB was the ONLY noncompliant city in OC. OC Registrar count TODAY ~5 PM.

**June 19 update:** General election confirmed: Becerra (D) vs Hilton (R). First poll: Becerra +21 points among likely voters. Democrats outnumber Republicans nearly 2-to-1 statewide. HB housing element adopted June 16 — HCD review pending. ⚡ D5: Dixon (R) takes lead over Foley (D) — could shift Board dynamics on state housing mandate enforcement.

**June 20 update:** General election confirmed: Becerra (D) vs Hilton (R) for governor; Valencia (D) vs Shader (R) for SD-34. HB housing element adopted June 16 — HCD review pending; councilmembers signaling continued resistance via ballot measures. D5: Dixon (R) leads Foley (D) — November race determines board majority.

**June 21 update:** Becerra (D) vs Hilton (R) confirmed for governor; Valencia (D) vs Shader (R) for SD-34. ⚡ D5 flipped — Foley (D) now leads Dixon (R). HB housing element adopted June 16 — fines at $50K/month, could increase to $150K/month next month. HB lost every legal challenge including US Supreme Court. Certification July 10.
active_campaigns.theory_of_change: State legislative races, especially primaries, are low-information elections where candidate questionnaires and voter guides have disproportionate influence. Most voters know nothing about state assembly candidates. A clear comparison of positions on housing, transit, and local government policy helps voters choose representatives who will strengthen (or weaken) the state mandate framework that OC housing advocacy depends on.
next_actions: Profile SD-34 candidates: Valencia (D) vs Shader (R) — compare housing mandate positions, Assembly voting records, Shader's 2022 run
Profile AD-68 candidates: Lopez (D) and Peñaloza (D) — compare Santa Ana council voting records on housing and development
Add Governor's race to tracking — THREE DEBATES: April 22 (Nexstar), April 28 (Pomona), May 5 (CNN). Evaluate candidates on housing enforcement posture (RHNA, Builder's Remedy, AG referrals)
Develop questionnaire focused on housing mandates, RHNA enforcement, Builder's Remedy, transit, and local control
Distribute questionnaires to SD-34, AD-68, and Governor candidates and publish all responses
Create comparison guides for all three races — SD-34, AD-68, and Governor
Track campaign finance filings — next filing April 23
Publish voter guides at least 4 weeks before June 2 primary

aligned_organizations.name: League of Women Voters Orange Coast
aligned_organizations.relationship: Primary partner for candidate forums and voter education. Nonpartisan credibility strengthens our accountability framing.

aligned_organizations.name: Voice of OC
aligned_organizations.relationship: Essential media partner. Their investigative journalism provides source material for scorecards, and our tracking data feeds their coverage.

aligned_organizations.name: OC Housing Now
aligned_organizations.relationship: Housing-focused ally. Our scorecards track housing votes; their advocacy uses the data to pressure non-compliant cities.

aligned_organizations.name: People for Housing OC
aligned_organizations.relationship: YIMBY ally — our housing vote tracking supports their advocacy campaigns.

aligned_organizations.name: Indivisible OC
aligned_organizations.relationship: Progressive grassroots ally. Their voter mobilization capacity amplifies our voter guide distribution.

aligned_organizations.name: OC Abundance Project
aligned_organizations.relationship: Cross-movement ally. Family formation policy depends on elected officials who support housing and childcare infrastructure. Our scorecards help identify allies.

engagement_pipeline.awareness: Scorecards shared on social media, Voice of OC coverage, election guide distribution through partner organizations. OC's razor-thin election margins make our data newsworthy.
engagement_pipeline.interest: Read a scorecard, attend a candidate forum, check your representatives' voting records. Understand how your city compares on housing and budget transparency.
engagement_pipeline.action: Vote informed using our guides. Share scorecards with neighbors. Attend a board or council meeting to observe your representatives in action. Submit public records requests. Contact your representatives about specific votes.
engagement_pipeline.leadership: Join the research team tracking votes and building scorecards. Lead tracking for a specific jurisdiction (City of Orange, Irvine, or Board of Supervisors). Coordinate candidate forums. Manage campaign finance monitoring.

immediate.action: Research all declared candidates for Board of Supervisors Districts 4 and 5
Voter registration deadline PASSED (May 18). Shift to GOTV — encourage early ballot return in D4/D5 for June 2 primary (13 DAYS). Every returned ballot matters for the Board majority
Share Board of Supervisors voter guide widely — ballots are already being cast. Focus social media push on D4 and D5 comparison content
Amplify Saddleback Meadows approval (May 6) as example of current Board's pro-development posture — contrast with what could change if Board flips

medium_term.action: Track early voting patterns and turnout data as they become available — identify low-turnout areas for targeted GOTV
Publish City of Orange council scorecard v1 — prepare for November council races (3 seats + mayor)
Post-primary analysis: evaluate June 2 results and develop November general election strategy
Begin collecting Irvine council meeting minutes for scorecard — 5-2 D majority at risk in November

strategic.action: Publish comprehensive November 2026 election guide covering City of Orange, City of Irvine, and all OC races
strategic.notes: This is the capstone product — a complete voter guide for every OC race that matters. Coordinate with all partner organizations for maximum distribution.

strategic.action: Establish ongoing council vote tracking infrastructure for permanent accountability
strategic.notes: Elections are episodic but accountability should be permanent. Build systems (volunteer rotation, data pipeline, publication schedule) that outlast individual election cycles.

strategic.action: Expand coverage to OCTA, school boards, and water districts in 2027
strategic.notes: Start with the highest-stakes bodies (BoS, city councils, state legislature) and expand as capacity allows. Special districts fly under the radar but make consequential decisions.

bridge_building.opposition_steelman: Swing-county voters often worry that accountability groups become partisan attack machines; they want fact-based oversight that checks all sides, protects public safety, and avoids ideological purity tests.

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Competent governance and oversight are conservative values when public money and authority are involved.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Competitive districts tend to reward candidates who demonstrate fiscal discipline and transparent performance tracking.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "This is not left or right—it is whether officials deliver results and disclose the receipts."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Accountability safeguards equity by making outcomes visible, not rhetorical.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Public scorecards can reveal gaps in service delivery across neighborhoods and demographics.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "We should judge leaders by measurable outcomes, not slogans."

cross_partisan_appeals.framing: Reduce noise; elevate verifiable facts and practical tradeoffs.
cross_partisan_appeals.evidence: Voter trust improves when organizations clearly separate evidence from opinion and update positions when facts change.
cross_partisan_appeals.example_dialogue: "If the data improves, we say so; if it worsens, we say so—regardless of party label."

common_ground: Officials should be judged on performance.
Public institutions should communicate clearly and honestly.
conversation_traps_to_avoid: Avoid nationalized partisan talking points disconnected from OC realities.
Avoid framing every disagreement as corruption.
Avoid overconfident predictions about election outcomes.
when_to_acknowledge_uncertainty: Unresolved legal disputes affecting implementation timelines.
honest_limits: We do not endorse every race or issue.
We cannot force agencies to release non-public data instantly.
We cannot guarantee that transparency alone changes voting behavior.
